question: A large enterprise with 100 AWS accounts needs to prevent any account from disabling CloudTrail or deleting CloudWatch Logs, even if the account administrator attempts to do so. The restriction must apply organization-wide. What is the MOST effective solution?
choices: Create a Service Control Policy (SCP) with explicit deny for cloudtrail:StopLogging and logs:DeleteLogGroup
Use IAM policies in each account to deny these actions
Enable AWS Config rules to detect and alert
Use Control Tower guardrails
answer: Create a Service Control Policy (SCP) with explicit deny for cloudtrail:StopLogging and logs:DeleteLogGroup
explanation: SCPs are organization-level policies that set maximum permissions for all accounts in AWS Organizations. An explicit deny in an SCP cannot be overridden by any IAM policy, even by account administrators. This is the most effective way to enforce organization-wide restrictions. The SCP prevents the actions from happening at all, regardless of IAM permissions.

why_not.Use IAM policies in each account to deny: Account administrators can modify or delete IAM policies within their own accounts. IAM policies are not sufficient for enforcing organization-wide restrictions because they can be changed by users with sufficient permissions. SCPs are managed at the organization level and cannot be modified by member accounts.
why_not.Enable AWS Config rules to detect and al: Config rules are detective controls that alert you after a violation occurs. They don't prevent the action from happening. By the time you receive an alert, CloudTrail might already be disabled or logs deleted. You need preventive controls (SCPs) that block the action before it happens.
why_not.Use Control Tower guardrails: While Control Tower guardrails can enforce similar restrictions, they're implemented using SCPs and Config rules under the hood. If you already have Organizations set up, directly creating an SCP is more straightforward. Control Tower is best for greenfield multi-account setup, not for adding specific restrictions to existing Organizations.

question: A company wants to set up a new multi-account AWS environment with pre-configured security guardrails and automated account provisioning based on AWS best practices. Which service automates this setup?
choices: AWS Control Tower
AWS Organizations
AWS Config
AWS CloudFormation
explanation: AWS Control Tower automates the setup of a secure, well-architected multi-account environment (landing zone) based on AWS best practices. It provides pre-configured guardrails (mandatory, strongly recommended, elective), Account Factory for self-service account provisioning, and a dashboard for governance. It's specifically designed for this use case.

why_not.AWS Organizations: Organizations provides the foundation for multi-account management (consolidated billing, SCPs, OUs), but it doesn't automate the setup of a landing zone or provide pre-configured guardrails. You'd need to manually configure SCPs, create OUs, and set up governance controls. Control Tower automates all of this.
why_not.AWS Config: Config monitors resource configurations for compliance but doesn't set up multi-account environments or provision accounts. It's a monitoring and compliance service, not an account setup and governance automation service. Config is used by Control Tower but doesn't provide the full landing zone automation.
why_not.AWS CloudFormation: CloudFormation is an infrastructure-as-code service for provisioning resources, but it doesn't specifically automate multi-account setup with security guardrails. You could build a custom solution with CloudFormation, but Control Tower provides this functionality out-of-the-box with AWS best practices built in.

question: A company has an SCP that allows all S3 actions and an IAM policy for a user that allows s3:PutObject but denies s3:DeleteObject. The user attempts to delete an S3 object. What will happen?
choices: The delete will be denied because the IAM policy explicitly denies it
The delete will be allowed because the SCP allows it
The delete will be denied because SCPs override IAM policies
The delete will be allowed because explicit allow in SCP overrides IAM deny
answer: The delete will be denied because the IAM policy explicitly denies it
explanation: An explicit deny anywhere in the policy evaluation chain always wins. Even though the SCP allows all S3 actions (doesn't restrict), the IAM policy has an explicit deny for s3:DeleteObject. Policy evaluation logic: explicit deny > explicit allow > implicit deny. The explicit deny in the IAM policy prevents the action, regardless of what the SCP allows.

why_not.The delete will be allowed because the S: SCPs set maximum permissions but don't override explicit denies in IAM policies. The effective permissions are the intersection of SCP and IAM policies, but explicit denies always win. Just because the SCP allows an action doesn't mean it will be permitted if an IAM policy explicitly denies it.
why_not.The delete will be denied because SCPs o: This is backwards. SCPs don't override IAM policies—they work together. SCPs set the maximum allowed permissions (boundary), and IAM policies work within that boundary. In this case, the deny comes from the IAM policy, not from the SCP overriding anything. The explicit deny in the IAM policy is what blocks the action.
why_not.The delete will be allowed because expli: Explicit deny ALWAYS wins, regardless of where the allow comes from. An SCP allow cannot override an IAM explicit deny. This is a fundamental rule of AWS policy evaluation. If there's an explicit deny anywhere (SCP, IAM policy, permission boundary, resource policy), the action is denied.

question: A company needs to continuously monitor all S3 buckets to ensure they have encryption enabled and are not publicly accessible. When a bucket becomes non-compliant, they want to receive notifications. Which service should they use?
choices: AWS Config with managed rules and SNS notifications
AWS CloudTrail with CloudWatch Alarms
Amazon GuardDuty
AWS Security Hub only
answer: AWS Config with managed rules and SNS notifications
explanation: AWS Config continuously evaluates resource configurations against rules. Use the managed rules 's3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled' and 's3-bucket-public-read-prohibited' to check compliance. Config can send SNS notifications when resources become non-compliant, providing the continuous monitoring and alerting required.

why_not.AWS CloudTrail with CloudWatch Alarms: CloudTrail logs API calls (like PutBucketPolicy or PutBucketEncryption) but doesn't continuously evaluate whether buckets are compliant with policies. You'd need to create complex metric filters and alarms for each configuration aspect. Config is purpose-built for continuous compliance monitoring.
why_not.Amazon GuardDuty: GuardDuty detects threats and malicious activity, not configuration compliance. It wouldn't alert on misconfigured bucket encryption or public access settings unless there was suspicious activity. GuardDuty is for threat detection, not configuration compliance monitoring.
why_not.AWS Security Hub only: Security Hub can display compliance status, but it requires AWS Config to actually perform the compliance checks. Security Hub aggregates findings from Config (and other sources) but doesn't do the continuous monitoring itself. You need Config enabled for Security Hub to show compliance status.

question: A company needs to download AWS compliance reports (SOC 2, PCI attestations) and sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) for HIPAA compliance. Which AWS service provides these documents?
choices: AWS Artifact
AWS Security Hub
AWS Config
AWS Trusted Advisor
explanation: AWS Artifact is a self-service portal for accessing AWS compliance reports, certifications, and agreements. You can download SOC reports, PCI attestations, ISO certifications, and sign BAAs for HIPAA compliance. It's the central repository for all AWS compliance documentation needed for audits.

why_not.AWS Security Hub: Security Hub provides security findings and compliance checks against standards like CIS and PCI-DSS, but it doesn't provide downloadable compliance reports or BAAs. Security Hub is for monitoring your security posture, not for accessing AWS's compliance documentation.
why_not.AWS Config: Config monitors your resource configurations for compliance with rules you define, but it doesn't provide AWS's compliance reports or certifications. Config is for checking your resources, not for accessing AWS's third-party audit reports or signing agreements.
why_not.AWS Trusted Advisor: Trusted Advisor provides recommendations for cost optimization, performance, security, and fault tolerance. While it has security checks, it doesn't provide compliance reports, certifications, or BAAs. Trusted Advisor is for operational recommendations, not compliance documentation.

question: A company uses AWS Organizations with multiple OUs (Production, Development, Sandbox). They want to prevent Sandbox accounts from using expensive services like Redshift and EMR, while allowing Production accounts full access. How should they implement this?
choices: Create an SCP that denies Redshift and EMR actions, apply it to the Sandbox OU only
Create IAM policies in each Sandbox account denying these services
Use AWS Budgets to alert when costs exceed threshold
Enable AWS Cost Explorer to monitor service usage
answer: Create an SCP that denies Redshift and EMR actions, apply it to the Sandbox OU only
explanation: SCPs can be applied to specific OUs, affecting only accounts within that OU. By creating an SCP with explicit denies for Redshift and EMR actions and applying it only to the Sandbox OU, you prevent all users in Sandbox accounts from using these services, regardless of their IAM permissions. Production accounts in other OUs remain unaffected.

why_not.Create IAM policies in each Sandbox acco: IAM policies can be modified by account administrators. If a developer in a Sandbox account has admin permissions, they could remove or modify the deny policy. SCPs are managed at the organization level and cannot be overridden by member accounts, making them more effective for enforcement.
why_not.Use AWS Budgets to alert when costs exce: Budgets are a detective control that alerts after costs are incurred. This doesn't prevent the use of expensive services—it only notifies you after the spending has occurred. You need a preventive control (SCP) to block the services before costs are incurred.
why_not.Enable AWS Cost Explorer to monitor serv: Cost Explorer is for analyzing and visualizing costs after they occur. Like Budgets, it's a detective control that doesn't prevent the use of services. It helps you understand spending patterns but doesn't enforce restrictions. You need SCPs for prevention.

question: A company needs to ensure all EC2 instances have approved AMIs and terminate any instances launched from unapproved AMIs. They want automated enforcement. Which solution provides this capability?
choices: AWS Config rule with automatic remediation to terminate non-compliant instances
Service Control Policy to deny launching unapproved AMIs
EventBridge rule to detect and terminate instances
AWS Systems Manager to scan and terminate instances
answer: AWS Config rule with automatic remediation to terminate non-compliant instances
explanation: AWS Config has a managed rule 'approved-amis-by-id' that checks if instances are launched from approved AMI IDs. You can configure automatic remediation to trigger a Systems Manager Automation document that terminates non-compliant instances. This provides continuous monitoring and automated enforcement.

why_not.Service Control Policy to deny launching: SCPs can't specify allowed AMI IDs—they work with services and actions, not resource-specific attributes like AMI IDs. You'd need to use IAM policies with conditions for AMI restrictions, but even then, it's preventive (blocks launch) rather than detective (terminates after launch). Config is better for this use case.
why_not.EventBridge rule to detect and terminate: EventBridge can detect EC2 instance launch events, but it doesn't have built-in logic to check if the AMI is approved. You'd need to write custom Lambda code to check AMI IDs and terminate instances. Config provides this functionality out-of-the-box with managed rules and automatic remediation.
why_not.AWS Systems Manager to scan and terminat: Systems Manager doesn't continuously monitor for compliance or automatically detect unapproved AMIs. It's a tool for executing actions, not for compliance monitoring. Config is the service that continuously evaluates compliance and can trigger Systems Manager for remediation.

question: A company has an organization with multiple accounts. They want to centrally manage and deploy security services (GuardDuty, Security Hub, Macie) across all accounts including new accounts created in the future. What is the MOST efficient approach?
choices: Designate a delegated administrator account and enable organization-wide integration
Manually enable services in each account
Use CloudFormation StackSets to deploy across accounts
Create Lambda function to enable services when accounts are created
answer: Designate a delegated administrator account and enable organization-wide integration
explanation: AWS security services support Organizations integration with delegated administrators. You designate one account as the delegated admin for each service, then enable organization-wide integration. This automatically enables the service in all existing and future accounts, with centralized management and aggregated findings. This is the native, most efficient approach.

why_not.Manually enable services in each account: Manual enablement doesn't scale and is error-prone. New accounts won't have services enabled automatically. You'd need to remember to enable services for each new account. Organizations integration automates this entirely, ensuring consistent security posture across all accounts.
why_not.Use CloudFormation StackSets to deploy a: While StackSets can deploy resources across accounts, it requires maintaining templates and manually adding new accounts to the stack set. Organizations integration is simpler and automatically includes new accounts. StackSets are better for custom resources, not for AWS security services that have native Organizations support.
why_not.Create Lambda function to enable service: This custom solution requires building, maintaining, and monitoring Lambda code. Organizations integration provides this functionality natively without custom code. Building custom automation when native integration exists adds unnecessary complexity and maintenance burden.

question: A company wants to prevent anyone in their organization from creating resources outside of us-east-1 and us-west-2 regions. What should they implement?
choices: Service Control Policy with deny for other regions
IAM policy in each account
AWS Config rule to detect resources in other regions
CloudWatch alarm for resources in other regions
answer: Service Control Policy with deny for other regions
explanation: SCPs can restrict which regions accounts can use by denying actions in unauthorized regions. Create an SCP with a condition that denies all actions except in us-east-1 and us-west-2. Apply it to the organization root or specific OUs. This prevents resource creation in other regions across all accounts, regardless of IAM permissions.

why_not.IAM policy in each account: IAM policies in individual accounts can be modified by account administrators. For organization-wide enforcement that can't be overridden, you need SCPs. Additionally, managing IAM policies across many accounts is inefficient compared to a single SCP.
why_not.AWS Config rule to detect resources in o: Config rules are detective—they alert after resources are created in unauthorized regions. They don't prevent creation. You need a preventive control (SCP) that blocks resource creation in unauthorized regions before it happens, not detection after the fact.
why_not.CloudWatch alarm for resources in other: CloudWatch alarms are reactive and notify after resources exist in unauthorized regions. They don't prevent creation. For preventive control that blocks actions before they happen, use SCPs. CloudWatch is for monitoring, not enforcement.

question: A company needs to track configuration changes to security groups and receive notifications when rules are modified. They want to know who made the change and when. Which combination of services provides this capability?
choices: AWS Config to track changes and SNS for notifications
CloudTrail to log API calls and CloudWatch for alarms
GuardDuty to detect changes
Security Hub to monitor security groups
answer: AWS Config to track changes and SNS for notifications
explanation: AWS Config continuously records configuration changes to resources including security groups. It captures what changed, when, and who made the change. Config can send notifications to SNS when resources change, providing real-time alerts. Config also maintains configuration history for compliance and auditing.

why_not.CloudTrail to log API calls and CloudWat: While CloudTrail logs the API calls (like AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress), it doesn't track the resulting configuration state or provide easy comparison of before/after configurations. Config is purpose-built for tracking configuration changes and provides better visibility into what actually changed in the security group rules.
why_not.GuardDuty to detect changes: GuardDuty detects threats and malicious activity, not configuration changes. It wouldn't generate findings for legitimate security group modifications. GuardDuty is for threat detection, not configuration change tracking. You need Config for configuration monitoring.
why_not.Security Hub to monitor security groups: Security Hub aggregates findings from other services but doesn't directly track configuration changes. It would show Config findings if Config is enabled, but Config is the service actually doing the tracking. Security Hub is for aggregation and prioritization, not for configuration change detection.
